div.head: 318. Information Memorandum From the President’s Special Assistant (Rostow) to President JohnsonSource: Johnson Library, National Security File, Rostow Files, Chlodnick File. Top Secret; Sensitive; Literally Eyes Only. The memorandum is marked with a “ps,” indicating that the President saw it.
div.p: Herewith an initial comment on Dobrynin’s position at the lunch he initiated.

list.item: Moscow is clearly ready to go—and eager—if you can work it out.

Their reasons are quite similar to our own: to create a good backdrop for the NPT in January; to keep the momentum of the work on missiles going into the next Administration; and, therefore, to avoid a long delay in both the NPT and the missile affairs.For CIA’s analysis of Soviet reasons for favoring an early start to missile talks, see Foreign Relations, 1964–1968, vol. XI, Document 296.
The heart of the matter, then, is not so much in the refinement of the principles, but in persuading Nixon that this is the right course for him, for the President, and for the nation.
